storm.summary_layperson: A single thunderstorm cell sits 38 miles to the north and is tracking eastward away from the cove — the main hazard tonight is the ongoing extreme heat event, not storms.
storm.local_effect_blurb: The cell approaches from inland to the north and never crosses Long Island Sound, so the cool 68°F Sound water offers no protective effect on this cell.
storm.detail_html:
A single convective cell is currently 38 miles to the north, registering a peak reflectivity of 36 dBZ on KOKX radar at 4:26 PM EDT. That puts it just above the moderate-risk threshold on reflectivity, which is why the risk is flagged moderate. However, the functional storm risk for the cove tonight is low: bulk wind shear is running 32–38 kt from the west-northwest, steering any organized cells eastward — away from Riverside, which sits due south of this cell.

Atmospheric instability is notably weak. HRRR reports only 130 J/kg of CAPE near the surface, and the RAP backup model gives 250 J/kg. Both are well below the 500 J/kg floor that supports sustained convection, and NWS Upton's Area Forecast Discussion contains no storm-intensity language for this evening. The one active alert is an Extreme Heat Watch (July 1–3) — a heat event, not a storm hazard.

The coastal stabilization mechanism is bypassed for this particular cell. A moderate marine layer is present over Long Island Sound — the inland-to-Sound temperature differential is 12°F — which can weaken convection that approaches from the southwest or northwest by forcing storms to cross cool water before reaching the cove. This cell is coming from the north overland and will never cross the Sound, so that protective effect is not in play. If the cell were to strengthen, regional NWS warnings should be taken at face value for Riverside.

Bottom line: moderate by the radar threshold, low by local dynamics. The cell is tracking east, instability is weak, and no severe warning is in effect. The main concern for the next 6–12 hours is the heat. Reassess if a new cell develops to the southwest — that approach angle would activate the Sound's protective mechanism and change the local picture.

hourly.shift_note: synoptic S holding post-sunset, thermal collapsed

wind.tactics_blurb: Light ESE–to–S sea breeze, 4–6 kt, with the onshore synoptic tail extending wind past the 8:25 PM sunset. Pressure sits on the west side of the course in ESE flow, shifting even as the breeze backs toward S — start mid-line and work the backing shift on port.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Today is a Regime A+ day: the inland–Sound temperature differential is 12.1°F (well above the 5°F thermal gate), and the background synoptic gradient is a light 6.5 kt from SE/SSE — onshore and reinforcing rather than opposing the sea breeze. In Regime A+, wind holds 30–60 minutes past sunset before fading, and the HRRR extended outlook confirms 3–5 kt S through 10 PM EDT. Sunset at the cove is 8:25 PM.

The sea breeze is only partially developed right now. The CT coast shows 67% of stations in southerly flow, but the Long Island north shore is at zero and the Sound buoy is reading ENE 4.6 kt — the Atlantic sea-breeze boundary has not yet crossed the Sound from the LI side. What we have is a thermally-driven local onshore flow off the Sound itself, reinforced by the synoptic southerly gradient. It's real, but it's not a fully-developed deep sea breeze. RYC is reading 3.1 kt 10-minute average at 4:32 PM with gusts to 7.4 — gusty relative to the mean, typical of an immature SB with thermal puffs.

Direction correction applied. HRRR is forecasting E 98° at 4 PM, but western-LIS stations are already at SE 129° — a 31° divergence. The maturation correction adds +15° to HRRR based on the deltaT and local hour. Corrected forecast direction is ESE 107–112° for 5–6 PM, backing to SE 137° around 8 PM and S 176° by 9 PM as the thermal collapses and synoptic flow takes over. Direction confidence is high — the obs–model divergence is large and unambiguous.

From a cove-geometry standpoint, ESE flow puts the course in partial shelter from Greenwich Point (Tod's Point to the east). The west shore has more consistent pressure; the east side near the Point will be lighter and shiftier. As the direction backs toward S by 8–9 PM, the cove opens more symmetrically and the pressure advantage levels out. In light, shifting 4–6 kt conditions, start mid-line to keep flexibility on both tacks.

Shift character through the window is persistent, not oscillating. The wind is making a one-way left rotation — ESE → SE → S — as the thermal matures and then transitions to synoptic flow. That's not the clean 10–20° oscillations of a fully-developed SB you can tack on mechanically; it's a progressive lift on port tack. Play the rotation by staying in phase with the backing shift rather than tacking on each small header. In 3–6 kt, small advantages compound — this is a race where clean air at the start and port-tack positioning on the first beat matter as much as side selection.

data_caveats: HRRR 22:00 EDT (fh=7) not in primary forecast window — speed extrapolated from extended outlook range (2.9–4.9 kt, mean 3.8 kt); direction estimated from post-sunset synoptic S trend
Storm cell motion not measured by pipeline — eastward track inferred from bulk shear direction (W/WNW 280°)
EW9544 Old Greenwich station offline (28,732 min stale) — excluded from ensemble
